title: Build vs hosted: governance decision + exception triage for private (VPC/on-prem) feasibility
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwoodinference.com>, Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwoodinference.com>, Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwoodinference.com>, Grace Oconnor <grace.oconnor@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Danielle Huang <d.huang@clearwave.health>
Date: 2028-05-03T09:14:00Z
Subject: Build vs hosted: governance decision + exception triage for private (VPC/on-prem) feasibility

Team —

Following our sync with ClearWave this morning, they pushed back on the hosted model and are asking for two options: a VPC-isolated hosting model in our cloud (customer VPC peering) or a co-located on-prem footprint. They’ve shared a high-level network diagram (attached) and an initial security questionnaire.

Id like a concise decision memo we can share with the customer by EOD Friday that covers:
- architecture feasibility (Karthik) — single-model vs split-model, latency implications, data flow
- ops & staffing delta (Soojin) — oncall, packaging, deployment automation needed for on-prem pilot
- legal/compliance exceptions we’d need to approve (Jonas / Grace) — SLA, data residency exceptions, audit windows

Attachments: priv-hosting-eval.xlsx (workbook with cost / ops worksheet), clearwave-network-diagram.png

Priority: customer is targeting Q3 pilot start; they expect a clear ask list and exception path.

Can people respond with their high-level blockers + a one-paragraph recommended path? I can consolidate.

Thanks —
Ava
Ava Chen
Director, Product Ops
Redwood Inference


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwoodinference.com>, Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwoodinference.com>, Grace Oconnor <grace.oconnor@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2028-05-03T11:02:00Z
Subject: Re: Build vs hosted: governance decision + exception triage for private (VPC/on-prem) feasibility

Thanks, Ava — quick architecture note from my side.

Short answer: both are feasible but with distinct trade-offs. High-level bullets:
- VPC (customer VPC peering / PrivateLink): fastest to pilot; isolates control plane + data paths if we limit model hosting to dedicated pools and enforce strict egress rules. Latency: +20-50ms compared to our shared-hosted instance depending on region.
- On-prem appliance (co-located): requires us to ship an image + runtime (k8s nodes or bare-metal) plus a secure updater. Latency: negligible; but major ops & security trade-offs.
- Model variant strategy: recommend a split-model approach for pilot — allow customer to run a quantized smaller model on-prem (to meet residency) while routing higher-capacity model calls to Redwood-hosted pools with explicit consent and contract terms. This limits engineering scope for a pilot.

Blockers / open questions I need:
1) ClearWave expected qps and peak concurrency (numbers) — drives dedicated pool sizing.
2) Acceptable model fidelity vs quantization (they must accept lower perf on on-prem variant).
3) Access pattern: will ClearWave want raw request logs or only audit logs?

Ill sketch a short diagram and update the workbook tomorrow.

—Karthik
Karthik Iyer
Principal Architect, Serving Runtime


From: Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwoodinference.com>, Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwoodinference.com>, Grace Oconnor <grace.oconnor@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2028-05-04T08:46:00Z
Subject: Re: Build vs hosted: governance decision + exception triage for private (VPC/on-prem) feasibility

Adding ops lens. TL;DR: VPC pilot is doable with small team; on-prem pilot requires cross-team bundling and will add ~2 FTEs for the pilot timeframe (release + packaging + oncall ramp).

Details:
- VPC flow: we leverage our Dedicated pools deployed in the vendor region and configure strict security groups, prefix lists, and egress NATs. Work: network runbook (~2 weeks), infra-as-code changes, testing harness for peered latency. Staffing: 0.5 SRE + 0.5 infra engineer during pilot (part-time) + product PM alignment.
- On-prem: we need a portable runtime build, signed artifacts, offline install instructions, a shrinkwrap packaging process, an airgap updater, and a runbook for customer ops. Estimate: 8–10 dev-weeks + 2 SRE FTE-months for pilot support.
- Serviceability: we need explicit telemetry gating (only health + audit logs) for on-prem unless legal approves log transfer.

Recommendation: propose a two-track pilot: VPC peering for immediate coverage; scoped on-prem pilot only if they pass a technical readout and sign an exception that limits remote debugging and defines a support window.

Attachment: I updated the Priv-hosting-eval.xlsx with the FTE math (see tab \"Ops delta\").

—Soojin
Soojin Lee
Head of Platform Ops


From: Danielle Huang <d.huang@clearwave.health>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwoodinference.com>, Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: ClearWave Legal <legal@clearwave.health>
Date: 2028-05-06T14:17:00Z
Subject: Re: Build vs hosted: governance decision + exception triage for private (VPC/on-prem) feasibility

Ava / team —

Thanks for the quick turnaround. A few clarifications from our side to help you size:
- Target pilot qps: 10 average, 120 peak for 5-minute windows. Typical payloads are 1–1.5k tokens.
- Data residency: PHI-bearing attributes must not leave our VPC unless fully anonymized and contractually permitted. We would prefer an on-prem deploy but are open to VPC peering if logs and raw request payloads stay inside our network.
- Timeline: wed like a pilot by end of Q3; thats firm per our internal roadmap.

We uploaded our network diagram and a short SOC extract; attaching the network diagram and a legal note (see attached). Happy to jump on a follow-up call next week to walk through the data flows.

—Danielle
Danielle Huang
Director, Cloud Platforms
ClearWave Health

To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwoodinference.com>, Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwoodinference.com>, Grace Oconnor <grace.oconnor@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Danielle Huang <d.huang@clearwave.health>
Date: 2028-05-10T16:22:00Z
Subject: Re: Build vs hosted: governance decision + exception triage for private (VPC/on-prem) feasibility

Quick legal & roadmap summary for the memo:
- Exceptions: We can sign a limited exception to allow a VPC-peered pilot where telemetry is scoped to health + aggregated metrics and where raw logs stay in customer-controlled storage. Any remote debugging requiring raw payloads must be opt-in per-incident and documented via a change control ticket.
- Contract: propose a 3-month pilot addendum (no SLA credits) and a follow-on review for full on-prem licensing if they want the appliance path.
- Risk: on-prem deployments raise IP leakage & support burden. We need an exception checklist executed by Security and Legal.

Next steps I will: draft the one-pager exception language and the pilot addendum; Ava can circulate. Karthik / Soojin — please confirm the small bullets above match your technical and ops constraints so I can include them in legal text.

Redwood Inference
text:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwoodinference.com>, Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwoodinference.com>, Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwoodinference.com>, Grace Oconnor <grace.oconnor@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Danielle Huang <d.huang@clearwave.health>
Date: 2028-05-03T09:14:00Z
Subject: Build vs hosted: governance decision + exception triage for private (VPC/on-prem) feasibility

Team —

Following our sync with ClearWave this morning, they pushed back on the hosted model and are asking for two options: a VPC-isolated hosting model in our cloud (customer VPC peering) or a co-located on-prem footprint. They’ve shared a high-level network diagram (attached) and an initial security questionnaire.

Id like a concise decision memo we can share with the customer by EOD Friday that covers:
- architecture feasibility (Karthik) — single-model vs split-model, latency implications, data flow
- ops & staffing delta (Soojin) — oncall, packaging, deployment automation needed for on-prem pilot
- legal/compliance exceptions we’d need to approve (Jonas / Grace) — SLA, data residency exceptions, audit windows

Attachments: priv-hosting-eval.xlsx (workbook with cost / ops worksheet), clearwave-network-diagram.png

Priority: customer is targeting Q3 pilot start; they expect a clear ask list and exception path.

Can people respond with their high-level blockers + a one-paragraph recommended path? I can consolidate.

Thanks —
Ava
Ava Chen
Director, Product Ops
Redwood Inference


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwoodinference.com>, Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwoodinference.com>, Grace Oconnor <grace.oconnor@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2028-05-03T11:02:00Z
Subject: Re: Build vs hosted: governance decision + exception triage for private (VPC/on-prem) feasibility

Thanks, Ava — quick architecture note from my side.

Short answer: both are feasible but with distinct trade-offs. High-level bullets:
- VPC (customer VPC peering / PrivateLink): fastest to pilot; isolates control plane + data paths if we limit model hosting to dedicated pools and enforce strict egress rules. Latency: +20-50ms compared to our shared-hosted instance depending on region.
- On-prem appliance (co-located): requires us to ship an image + runtime (k8s nodes or bare-metal) plus a secure updater. Latency: negligible; but major ops & security trade-offs.
- Model variant strategy: recommend a split-model approach for pilot — allow customer to run a quantized smaller model on-prem (to meet residency) while routing higher-capacity model calls to Redwood-hosted pools with explicit consent and contract terms. This limits engineering scope for a pilot.

Blockers / open questions I need:
1) ClearWave expected qps and peak concurrency (numbers) — drives dedicated pool sizing.
2) Acceptable model fidelity vs quantization (they must accept lower perf on on-prem variant).
3) Access pattern: will ClearWave want raw request logs or only audit logs?

Ill sketch a short diagram and update the workbook tomorrow.

—Karthik
Karthik Iyer
Principal Architect, Serving Runtime


From: Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwoodinference.com>, Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwoodinference.com>, Grace Oconnor <grace.oconnor@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2028-05-04T08:46:00Z
Subject: Re: Build vs hosted: governance decision + exception triage for private (VPC/on-prem) feasibility

Adding ops lens. TL;DR: VPC pilot is doable with small team; on-prem pilot requires cross-team bundling and will add ~2 FTEs for the pilot timeframe (release + packaging + oncall ramp).

Details:
- VPC flow: we leverage our Dedicated pools deployed in the vendor region and configure strict security groups, prefix lists, and egress NATs. Work: network runbook (~2 weeks), infra-as-code changes, testing harness for peered latency. Staffing: 0.5 SRE + 0.5 infra engineer during pilot (part-time) + product PM alignment.
- On-prem: we need a portable runtime build, signed artifacts, offline install instructions, a shrinkwrap packaging process, an airgap updater, and a runbook for customer ops. Estimate: 8–10 dev-weeks + 2 SRE FTE-months for pilot support.
- Serviceability: we need explicit telemetry gating (only health + audit logs) for on-prem unless legal approves log transfer.

Recommendation: propose a two-track pilot: VPC peering for immediate coverage; scoped on-prem pilot only if they pass a technical readout and sign an exception that limits remote debugging and defines a support window.

Attachment: I updated the Priv-hosting-eval.xlsx with the FTE math (see tab \"Ops delta\").

—Soojin
Soojin Lee
Head of Platform Ops

title: Production degradation — credit and trial extension request
preview:
From: Jordan Park <ops@luminedge.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>
Cc: Priya Shah <priya@luminedge.com>
Date: Tue, 07 May 2028 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Production incident — May 6-7 token and latency spike

Hi Aditya,

We saw repeated 5-10s tail latencies and token throttles across our nightly inference jobs between 2028-05-06 22:30 UTC and 2028-05-07 03:15 UTC. This manifested as elevated error rates and several timeouts for customer-facing flows. We logged it as INC-2497 on our side.

Can you share what happened on Redwood's side, whether you have remediation logs, and whether there's scope for a credit/compensation for the impacted tokens? The outage caused a visible regression for a subset of our customers and we had to route traffic to a fallback.

Thanks — we need a ballpark by EOD to brief our leadership.

Jordan Park
Site Reliability Manager, LuminEdge
ops@luminedge.com

Attachments: (none)


From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Park <ops@luminedge.com>, Priya Shah <priya@luminedge.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 07 May 2028 09:38:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Production incident — May 6-7 token and latency spike

Jordan — thanks for the heads up and really sorry for the disruption. We flagged increased queueing on a routing tier (service mesh + autoscale boundary) that coincided with a model reload; routing fallback hit slower CPU paths for ~4 hours.

Immediate next steps from our side:
- Postmortem in progress (P0) and I’ll share the findings tomorrow.
- We can apply an immediate token safety-net credit while we finish the recap (to minimize your billing shock).
- I’m proposing a preliminary offer: 125,000 free tokens + a 30-day trial extension on your current onboarding entitlement while we investigate. If that looks insufficient once we finish the analysis, we can adjust.

If that proposed remediation sounds ok, I’ll route for quick approval from pricing/ops and send a formal credit note.

— Aditya
Aditya Rao
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
aditya.rao@redwood.com

> We saw repeated 5-10s tail latencies and token throttles...

Subject: Re: Production incident — May 6-7 token and latency spike

Thanks for the flag, Aditya. I reviewed the initial notes from infra — the incident aligns with a model reload edge-case that temporarily increased CPU-bound fallbacks. Marissa — please prepare a credit note and schedule the credit to appear in their billing (apply as account-level token credit, not invoice refund). Sean, please coordinate any SLA note if they ask for additional uptime credits.

Karthik
VP, Commercial Operations
karthik.iyer@redwood.com

- credits_schedule.csv (applies the 150000 tokens to their account on 2028-05-11)
- DocuSign amendment link if they want the trial extension documented for procurement.

I’ll send for signatures and copy legal if they request line-item changes.

Marissa Cole
Sales Operations, Redwood Inference

Subject: Resolution: LuminEdge — credit applied and trial extension

Thanks for your patience while we investigated. Per our internal review, the root cause was a routing/autoscale interaction during a model reload window. We’ve implemented an immediate mitigation and the infra team is finalizing a hardening patch (ETA 5/14).

What we’ve applied now:
- 150,000 token account credit (applied as of 2028-05-11; see attached credits_schedule.csv).

Best,

title: Recap + price targets from today's call — token economics readout vs OpenAI/hosts
preview:
From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
To: Anya Patel <anya@finlytics.com>, Samir Khan <samir@finlytics.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 4 May 2028 14:03:00 -0700
Subject: Recap + price targets from today's call — token economics readout vs OpenAI/hosts

Thanks again for the time today — great conversation. Quick written recap and the pricing signals we discussed so the team has one place to reference. Please flag anything I've misunderstood.

Call highlights
- Primary use case: realtime summarization + routing for customer support tickets (mix of short prompts + long context).
- Expected volume (next 12 months): 15M–25M tokens/month; pilot expected at ~2M tokens/day for 30 days.
- Latency SLO: 95th <= 350ms for single-turn summaries.
- Need: clear unit economics to validate a < $400k ARR procurement threshold.

Customer budget signal (from Samir/Anya):
- Comfortable monthly spend range: $20k–$40k/mo initially (scale plan after pilot).
- Finance target: implied $/1M tokens target roughly $6–$10 if coverage and SLA can be met.

Competitive price signals (rough from our public/partner intel — shared for context):
- OpenAI (hosted completions/chat endpoint): ~ $18–$22 per 1M tokens equivalent for their current gen (note: varies by model & prompt mix).
- Anthropic (hosted): ~ $15–$19 per 1M tokens equivalent.
- Hugging Face / hosted inference providers: ~ $11–$14 per 1M tokens (depends on model and batching).
- Self-host (customer-managed infra): infra-only marginal cost estimate ~ $4–$6 per 1M tokens but requires ops + reserved GPU costs + engineering.

Initial Redwood positioning (what I proposed on call):
- Pilot offer: dedicated reserved pool + pilot SOW, 30 days, up to 2M tokens/day.
- Hosted API (no commitment): list/standard pricing higher; not ideal for budget constrained pilots.
- Dedicated reserved pricing (sample): $8 per 1M tokens effective at 20–25M/mo commit (includes prefix caching and batch-config optimizations).
- Enterprise private deployment: can approach self-host marginals if customer takes on infra and ops (lower $/1M but with different tradeoffs).

Next steps I promised:
1) Share a concise 1M-token price grid (hosted vs dedicated vs private) — attached as a first pass.
2) Draft pilot SOW + acceptance criteria (attached).
3) Quick legal/procurement checklist — Ben, can you scope likely discount/contract cadence for a 12mo commit if Finlytics moves forward?
4) Internal: engineering to confirm latency numbers on their average prompt shape (Lauren is looped).

Attachments included: Finlytics_RFP_Q3.pdf (their RFP), Finlytics_budget_Q3.xlsx (their budget input that Samir shared).

If that all looks right I'll follow up with the formal price grid and a short slide (1 page) comparing Redwood effective $/1M vs the hosts we discussed.

Thanks — talk soon.

-- 
Marcus Lin
Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference
marcus.lin@redwood.com
+1 (415) 555-0192


From: Anya Patel <anya@finlytics.com>
To: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Cc: Samir Khan <samir@finlytics.com>
Date: Thu, 4 May 2028 15:12:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Recap + price targets from today's call — token economics readout vs OpenAI/hosts

Marcus — thanks for the writeup, that matches our takeaways. A couple of clarifications / asks:

- Confirm the $8 per 1M example you mentioned is for a dedicated reserved pool and assumes 20–25M tokens/mo commit?
- Our CFO wants the pilot (30 days) to run with a capped spend of $45k and a path to a 12-month committed price if pilot hits SLA. Can you provide the simple banded price tiers we discussed (pilot, 6mo, 12mo)?
- We'd like a short one-pager that shows Redwood vs OpenAI/Anthropic/HF hosting at 1M, 10M, 25M monthly volumes (effective $/1M after discounts). That will feed into our procurement spreadsheet.

I've attached our budget worksheet and the internal RFP excerpt (Finlytics_budget_Q3.xlsx, Finlytics_RFP_Q3.pdf).

If you can get the price grid and a 1-page competitive readout by EOD tomorrow, we'll route to procurement for an initial OK to engage legal.

Best,
Anya
Head of Product
Finlytics
anya@finlytics.com


From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
To: Anya Patel <anya@finlytics.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 4 May 2028 16:30:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Recap + price targets from today's call — token economics readout vs OpenAI/hosts

Thanks Anya — attaching the two artifacts we discussed: redwood_1M_price_grid_v1.xlsx and pilot_SOW_draft.pdf. Short summary of the grid below (effective $ per 1M tokens, illustrative):

- Pilot (no commit, capped): $14 / 1M (pilot discount built in, includes monitoring + 30-day SOW). Pilot capped at $45k as requested.
- 6-month partial commit (10–15M/mo): $10 / 1M effective (tiered usage).
- 12-month commit (20–25M/mo): $8 / 1M effective (includes prefetched KV cache and priority routing).

Competitive framing (effective $/1M, approximate):
- OpenAI hosted: $18–22 / 1M (depends on model; gen-accuracy premium).
- Anthropic hosted: $15–19 / 1M.
- Hosted HF / other managed hosts: $11–14 / 1M.
- Self-hosted (customer managed infra): $4–6 / 1M but non-trivial ops and infra capex.

Notes / assumptions:
- Numbers assume typical call profile (60% short prompts, 40% long context) and our caching/batching suggestions (we include KV caching that reduces cost by ~15% on observed workloads).
- We can tighten SLAs and include higher SLO credits in the 12-month commit band if procurement needs that.

Immediate next steps (proposed timeline):
1) Finlytics signs pilot SOW (30-day pilot, capped at $45k) — we provision a dedicated pool for the pilot.
2) Pilot monitoring + weekly checkpoints (SLA check at day 21) — if pass, move to commit negotiation.
3) Legal/procurement: Ben will drive discounts/contract cadence; I'll hand off the SOW and the price_grid spreadsheet to legal.

Links: HubSpot deal HS-89342, Drive folder with attachments above.

Anything else you want in the one-pager? I can add an explicit per-volume table for 1M/10M/25M if that helps — happy to include.

-- 
Marcus Lin
Enterprise Sales, Redwood Inference


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 4 May 2028 17:48:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Recap + price targets from today's call — token economics readout vs OpenAI/hosts

Adding legal & procurement playbook notes:
- For a $45k capped pilot + target ARR < $400k we should be able to offer the 12-month anchor at $8/1M with a 60–75 day procurement window (standard external procurement timelines).
- I'll ask Legal for a fast-track NDA + pilot SOW review and flag potential discount layering if Finlytics wants a clause limiting infra residency to US-only.

text:
From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
To: Anya Patel <anya@finlytics.com>, Samir Khan <samir@finlytics.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 4 May 2028 14:03:00 -0700
Subject: Recap + price targets from today's call — token economics readout vs OpenAI/hosts

Thanks again for the time today — great conversation. Quick written recap and the pricing signals we discussed so the team has one place to reference. Please flag anything I've misunderstood.

Call highlights
- Primary use case: realtime summarization + routing for customer support tickets (mix of short prompts + long context).
- Expected volume (next 12 months): 15M–25M tokens/month; pilot expected at ~2M tokens/day for 30 days.
- Latency SLO: 95th <= 350ms for single-turn summaries.
- Need: clear unit economics to validate a < $400k ARR procurement threshold.

Customer budget signal (from Samir/Anya):
- Comfortable monthly spend range: $20k–$40k/mo initially (scale plan after pilot).
- Finance target: implied $/1M tokens target roughly $6–$10 if coverage and SLA can be met.

Competitive price signals (rough from our public/partner intel — shared for context):
- OpenAI (hosted completions/chat endpoint): ~ $18–$22 per 1M tokens equivalent for their current gen (note: varies by model & prompt mix).
- Anthropic (hosted): ~ $15–$19 per 1M tokens equivalent.
- Hugging Face / hosted inference providers: ~ $11–$14 per 1M tokens (depends on model and batching).
- Self-host (customer-managed infra): infra-only marginal cost estimate ~ $4–$6 per 1M tokens but requires ops + reserved GPU costs + engineering.

Initial Redwood positioning (what I proposed on call):
- Pilot offer: dedicated reserved pool + pilot SOW, 30 days, up to 2M tokens/day.
- Hosted API (no commitment): list/standard pricing higher; not ideal for budget constrained pilots.
- Dedicated reserved pricing (sample): $8 per 1M tokens effective at 20–25M/mo commit (includes prefix caching and batch-config optimizations).
- Enterprise private deployment: can approach self-host marginals if customer takes on infra and ops (lower $/1M but with different tradeoffs).

Next steps I promised:
1) Share a concise 1M-token price grid (hosted vs dedicated vs private) — attached as a first pass.
2) Draft pilot SOW + acceptance criteria (attached).
3) Quick legal/procurement checklist — Ben, can you scope likely discount/contract cadence for a 12mo commit if Finlytics moves forward?
4) Internal: engineering to confirm latency numbers on their average prompt shape (Lauren is looped).

Attachments included: Finlytics_RFP_Q3.pdf (their RFP), Finlytics_budget_Q3.xlsx (their budget input that Samir shared).

If that all looks right I'll follow up with the formal price grid and a short slide (1 page) comparing Redwood effective $/1M vs the hosts we discussed.

Thanks — talk soon.

-- 
Marcus Lin
Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference
marcus.lin@redwood.com
+1 (415) 555-0192

On it — initial findings below. Will follow up with a short table for the deck.

Preliminary QA:
- 95th percentile latency for sequences >1024 tokens: +38% (baseline week-over-week).
- 99th percentile: +72% (spikes correlate with batch sizes hitting autoscale boundaries).

Mitigation plan (short):

3) Longer-term: schedule a quantization rollback / retune and run a 48-hr soak test.

title: Retention parachute & surge cushion proposal — Olive Labs renewal
preview:
From: Sara Nunez <sara@olivelabs.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: Ops Team <ops@olivelabs.com>
Date: 2028-04-20T15:10:00-07:00
Subject: Renewal conversation — capacity needs + pricing ask

Hi Ben,

Hope you’re well. Our renewal for Aug 1 is coming up and usage has materially increased after the Q1 product launch. We’re expecting a 2.5x spike in inference hours for the next two quarters and need to lock predictable economics before committing to a longer term.

Key asks:
- A short-term rate hold for the next 3 months while we onboard new models
- A surge cushion (either credits or capped overage rates) to smooth unexpected spikes
- Clear path for committed capacity increase without lengthy reprocurement

Can we get a sketch of what Redwood can offer? Happy to schedule 30m next week.

Thanks,
Sara Nunez
Head of Platform Ops
Olive Labs

Attachment: olive_usage_snapshot_Mar2028.csv (CSV)

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Sara Nunez <sara@olivelabs.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-04-21T09:24:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Renewal conversation — capacity needs + pricing ask

Sara — thanks for the note and congrats on the strong Q1 launch. We can absolutely put together a retention-focused option that avoids you getting dinged for short-term growth while also protecting our capacity economics.

High-level proposal (sketch):
- 3-month "parachute" rate hold: existing rates preserved for 90 days from renewal effective date
- Surge cushion: 250k token-credit (or equivalent $ value) to absorb spikes, followed by a capped overage of 15% discount to our normal overage rate for the remainder of the first quarter
- Committed uplift path: ability to add committed GPU hours with 30-day notice; price ladder defined in a term sheet

If that sounds directionally right, I’ll loop in Monica to formalize a term sheet and share a workbook with cost scenarios. Can you do Wed 4/24 10:00am PT for a quick alignment?

—Ben
Ben Carter | Account Executive, Redwood Inference
Attachment: (none)

On 2028-04-20 15:10, Sara Nunez wrote:
> Hi Ben,
> 
> Hope you’re well. Our renewal for Aug 1 is coming up...

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-04-22T11:05:00-07:00
Subject: Fwd: Renewal conversation — capacity needs + pricing ask (Olive Labs)

Monica / Marissa — forwarding Olive Labs' ask. High-level they want a short-term rate protection + surge credits and an easy path to expand commits.

My suggested commercial approach (please advise):
- 90-day parachute rate hold (preserve current per-token rate) tied to a conditional renewal signature today (term to start Aug 1)
- Surge cushion = 250k token-credits or $40k credit (which is more practical?)
- Overages during the 90-day window billed at existing rate but capped with a 15% discount post-credit exhaustion
- Option to add dedicated capacity with 30 days’ notice; discounts laddered by committed tier

Questions for Pricing/Ops:
1) Is a 250k token-credit conservative/too large given their usage file?
2) Can we formalize the 30-day uplift path without rearchitecting their deployment?

title: Next steps: HorizonCare integration & routing policy checklist
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Liam O'Neill <liam.oneill@horizoncare.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>, Connor OBrien <connor.obrien@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 24 Apr 2028 20:12:00 -0700
Subject: Recap + immediate asks from today's call (HorizonCare)

Hi Liam / Priya —

Thanks again for the call today. Quick recap and suggested next steps so we have an actionable onboarding plan. High-level items from our end:

- Integration surface: you'll embed Redwood's SDK into your inference gateway (Node/Go) and call the hosted endpoint for text generation, embeddings, and the reranker.
- Auth choices: you indicated preference for API keys for the sandbox and mTLS-backed tokens for production.
- Routing policy: primary model for user-facing assistant will be redwood-llama-13b-q8; fallback chain to redwood-7b-quant when latency spikes/cost SLOs hit.
- Region constraints: production needs US-West + EU fallback for EU users (data residency request for EU traffic).
- Structured output/tool calling: Planner component will use function-calling; we should define schemas for the top 6 actions (create-ticket, lookup-patient, summarize-note, recommend-codes, escalate).
- Embeddings/rerank: use embeddings for cross-document retrieval (semantic search) and lightweight in-memory reranker for UI autocomplete.

Immediate asks:
1) Please share a short list of the top 50 prompt patterns and the representative latency SLOs you'd accept for each class (e.g., chat-completion: 500ms p95; long summarize: 2.5s p95).
2) Confirm whether HorizonCare will provide a VPC peering + allowlisted egress IPs for the private deployment or whether you prefer Dedicated hosting.
3) Confirm who will be on the integration workshop (engineer + security + product).

Attached: sandbox creds (redwood-sandbox-creds.txt) and a suggested architecture sketch (redwood-arch-diagram.pdf). I've also uploaded a simple QA spreadsheet for mapping prompt families to routing policy buckets (horizoncare-integration-qa.xlsx).

Next steps from Redwood: Monica will run a 90-minute integration workshop and Connor will draft the routing policy template.

Best,
Ben Carter
Senior Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai
+1 (415) 555-0123

---
Attachments: redwood-sandbox-creds.txt (text), redwood-arch-diagram.pdf (pdf), horizoncare-integration-qa.xlsx (spreadsheet)


From: Liam O'Neill <liam.oneill@horizoncare.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Priya Sharma <priya.sharma@horizoncare.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 25 Apr 2028 09:10:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Recap + immediate asks from today's call (HorizonCare)

Thanks Ben — this is great, appreciate the clarity. A few clarifications from our side:

- Latency SLOs: for chat flows we target p95 <= 600ms for short-turn QA, and p95 <= 2s for long-document summarizes. We'll be pragmatic on the first pass.
- Auth: we prefer mTLS + short-lived tokens for prod. Sandbox API keys are fine for dev. Security will need the cert/key exchange steps documented.
- Data residency: EU traffic must be pinned to EU region for three endpoints (search, summarize, patient-retrieve). Can Redwood provide per-endpoint region pins?
- Fallback behavior: we want a cost-first fallback chain (route to cheaper model if cost threshold breached) but only if quality drop is acceptable; we should be able to configure thresholds per route.

Action items on our side:
- Priya will collect the sample prompts and the user distribution that should feed into routing buckets.
- I will check with infra on VPC peering — likely yes for prod, sandbox will use public endpoints.

Can we lock a workshop for next Tue (30 Apr) 10am PT? Monica / Connor mentioned they'd run one.

Thanks,
Liam
Director of Product, HorizonCare
liam.oneill@horizoncare.com


From: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>
To: Liam O'Neill <liam.oneill@horizoncare.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Connor OBrien <connor.obrien@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 25 Apr 2028 16:45:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Recap + immediate asks from today's call (HorizonCare)

Liam — thanks for the note. Tue 30 Apr 10am PT works for us; I've tentatively booked a 90-minute slot and sent a calendar invite to the list.

A few technical pre-reads I'll circulate before the workshop:
- SDK quickstart (Node & Go) with sample mTLS and token exchange flows.
- Routing policy template that covers: primary model, fallback chain, latency and cost thresholds, region pinning, and per-route quality gates.
- Function schemas for the top 6 actions — we'll ask product to validate them during the workshop.

Connor: can you prep an initial routing-policy YAML that maps 3 prompt classes to model targets and includes a simple cost-based fallback rule? Put it in the Drive folder and I'll link it in the invite.

Liam: please upload the prompt examples to the Drive folder (top-level doc) and flag any regulatory constraints for EU pinned endpoints.

Talk soon,
Monica Patel
Solutions Engineering Lead | Redwood Inference
monica.patel@redwood.ai


From: Connor OBrien <connor.obrien@redwood.ai>
To: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Liam O'Neill <liam.oneill@horizoncare.com>
Date: Fri, 26 Apr 2028 09:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Recap + immediate asks from today's call (HorizonCare)

Quick update from my side — initial routing-policy YAML uploaded to the Drive folder: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1a2bHorizonCareOnboard (see file redwood-routing-policy-horizoncare.yaml). Highlights:

- Prompt classes mapped: quick-chat, patient-summary, batch-embeddings retrieval.
- Primary targets: redwood-llama-13b-q8 (quick-chat & summary), redwood-embed-1 (embeddings).
- Fallback: redwood-7b-quant (cost-fallback) with dynamic eligibility when 5-min cost projection > $0.12 per request (approx).
- Region pinning: route requests with EU user-region header to eu-redwood-llama-13b-q8; we included an allowlist check for EU-only datasets before pinging EU region.
- Function calling schemas attached as comments for review.

Next concrete items I will complete by Mon 29 Apr:
1) Sandbox example (curl + Node snippet) showing mTLS setup and function-calling sample.
2) A sample test harness that runs prompt families through primary and fallback models and records latency/cost metrics.

Monica/Ben: let me know if you want me to include an opinionated quantization recommendation for the fallback model (it reduces cost but we need to call out quality regressions).

Thanks,
Connor OBrien
Platform Engineer | Redwood Inference
connor.obrien@redwood.ai

title: Cross-entity replay charges — request for split allocation, VAT treatment, and corrected invoice
preview:
From: Elena Rossi <accounts-payable@acmerobotics.com>
To: Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwood.com>
Cc: Acme Robotics Finance <finance@acmerobotics.com>
Date: Thu, 12 Apr 2028 09:17:00 -0400
Subject: Invoice INV-20280412 hold — large embed replay charges and VAT classification

Hi Logan,

We received invoice INV-20280412 and have placed payment on hold. On line items 4-6 there are ~1.2M embed-replay units billed to Acme Robotics that our procurement team does not recognize — the usage appears to have originated from a different Acme entity (APAC subsidiary) and should not be charged to our EU entity. Separately, the VAT line shows 20% applied to the full amount; because the traffic was cross-border and belongs to an APAC buyer, we believe EU reverse-charge should apply (no VAT on the invoice).

Can you provide: 1) the raw usage export CSV for 2028-04-12 through 2028-04-15 with prefix and entity tags; 2) an explanation of why the replay traffic was attributed to Acme Robotics EU; 3) timeline for a corrected invoice or credit note so we can release the payment?

Attachments: INV-20280412-acme.pdf (invoice), usage_export_20280412.csv (sample from AP events)

Thanks — we need this resolved for our month-end.

Best,
Elena
Accounts Payable
Acme Robotics


From: Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwood.com>
To: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: 
Date: Thu, 12 Apr 2028 10:04:00 -0400
Subject: Fwd: Invoice INV-20280412 hold — large embed replay charges and VAT classification

Kimberly / Ben — forwarding a hold from Acme AP regarding a ~1.2M embed-replay charge they claim belongs to a different Acme entity (APAC). They also dispute VAT. Can you do a quick allocation + VAT triage?

Quick notes: I pulled the usage export link and attached the invoice they sent. On first pass I see a high concentration of requests with prefix \"acme-apac-ingest\" that were processed under Acme's EU billing tag; this looks like a routing/tenant-tagging issue during a temporary migration window.

What I need from you for reply to Elena:
- Confirm whether we can reattribute those units to Acme APAC (give exact unit counts)
- Propose a credit note or corrected invoice (legal/tax input may be needed)
- ETA for corrected invoicing so they can release payment (they flagged month-end)

If you need me to loop in Tax/Legal please say and I will escalate.

Thanks,
Logan
Senior Revenue Operations
Redwood Inference


From: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
To: Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: 
Date: Fri, 13 Apr 2028 08:21:00 -0400
Subject: Re: Fwd: Invoice INV-20280412 hold — large embed replay charges and VAT classification

Thanks Logan — I pulled the full usage export and did a short audit. Summary:
- Count: 1,204,380 embed-replay units between 2028-04-12T02:00Z and 2028-04-15T18:00Z.
- Attribution: 98% of those events have tenant_prefix=acme-apac-ingest and origin_region=ap-southeast-1, but our billing ingestion used the EU billing_tag (acme-eu-prod) for that window due to a short-lived tenant-tag sync failure during the deployment on 2028-04-12.
- Root cause: a config roll that reset tenant-tag mappings in the router; affected requests were routed to an EU routing pool that stamped the EU billing tag.

Proposal:
1) Reallocate 1,204,380 embed-replay units from Acme EU to Acme APAC in our billing ledger (apply to their APAC account).
2) Issue a corrected invoice to Acme EU removing those lines and a credit note for the overcharge (proposed credit attached).
3) For VAT: recommend treating the reallocated traffic as supplied to an APAC entity — issue credit without EU VAT and annotate reverse-charge where appropriate. We should loop in Tax for formal sign-off; I can draft a one-paragraph rationale for their records.

Attachments: proposed_credit_note_ACME-8372.pdf, allocation_evidence_20280412.csv

If we can get Tax sign-off today I can generate the credit note and request Finance to reissue a corrected invoice by COB Monday. Ben — can you confirm Finance is OK with an expedited credit and whether we should hold any penalty waivers?

Kimberly
Billing & Revenue Ops
Redwood Inference


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>, Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwood.com>
Cc: 
Date: Fri, 13 Apr 2028 09:02:00 -0400
Subject: Re: Fwd: Invoice INV-20280412 hold — large embed replay charges and VAT classification

Appreciate the audit, Kim. From Finance side:
- We can reallocate and issue a credit note for the EU invoice; no problem expediting.

title: Site anchoring & evacuation window — dedicated region assurance
preview:
From: Laura Perez <lperez@fintrust.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 22 Apr 2028 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Site anchoring & evacuation window — dedicated region assurance

Hi Ben,

Thanks for the demo last week. As we scope our Dedicated deployment for FinTrust's retail payments workloads, security is asking for clarity on three operational commitments related to region anchoring:

1) Physical/tenant anchoring: can you confirm that customer-backed Dedicated pools are restricted to a single geographic region (no automatic cross-region snapshots or replication without customer opt-in)?

2) Evacuation/maintenance window: what is the expected mean time to route traffic off a pinned site during urgent maintenance or an incident? We need an operational window we can commit to our internal SLAs (ideally an upper bound in hours).

3) Evidence/artifacts: what artifacts can Redwood provide to show isolation (network topology map, sanitized audit logs, snapshot locations, and an ops playbook)? Our SOC2 vendor will need examples.

I've attached our high-level residency questionnaire and a sketch of the requirement. Can you send an initial response and a proposed delivery timeline?

Thanks,

Laura Perez
Director, Infrastructure Security
FinTrust Bank
attachments: fintrust-data-residency-questionnaire.xlsx

--
On Fri, Apr 22, 2028 at 09:12 AM Laura Perez <lperez@fintrust.com> wrote:
> [Attachment: fintrust-data-residency-questionnaire.xlsx]

From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Laura Perez <lperez@fintrust.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.com>, Dev Patel <dev_patel@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 22 Apr 2028 10:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Site anchoring & evacuation window — dedicated region assurance

Hi Laura — really appreciate the clear asks. Quick responses inline below, and I'll follow up with the artifacts we can share.

1) Anchoring: For Dedicated pools we offer region-anchored provisioning. Customer-reserved capacity is pinned at cluster/zone level and by default we do not replicate persistent customer snapshots to a different region unless the customer explicitly enables cross-region backup. Backups are written to the pinned region S3 bucket under customer-controlled encryption keys (we can use your KMS).

2) Evacuation/maintenance window: Typical planned evacuations (maintenance with live migration) complete within 1–3 hours for re-routing traffic, depending on flow sizes; for urgent hardware failures we trigger automatic failover to warm standby capacity in the same region where possible, and to a pre-agreed secondary region only if you opt in to cross-region DR. For FinTrust-grade SLAs we can commit to an operational target of 4 hours for full traffic re-routing in emergencies (this is the upper bound we propose for contractual language).

3) Evidence: We can produce: (a) a sanitized network topology map showing tenancy boundaries, (b) a redacted ops playbook (evacuation-playbook_v2.pdf), (c) audit log extracts for a scrubbed tenant (timestamped events showing snapshot writes/erases), and (d) a one-page attestation (redwood-region-attestation.docx) that describes retention/replication behavior. I've attached the playbook and will share the attestation after legal review.

Next steps: Kimberly and Dev will pull the topology map and sanitized logs. Can we schedule a 30-min walk-through the week of Apr 25 so we can review the artifacts with your security and compliance folks?

Regards,
Ben Carter
Senior Account Executive
Redwood Inference
attachments: evacuation-playbook_v2.pdf

--
On Fri, Apr 22, 2028 at 10:05 AM Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com> wrote:
> Attached: evacuation-playbook_v2.pdf

From: Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.com>
To: Laura Perez <lperez@fintrust.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Dev Patel <dev_patel@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 24 Apr 2028 08:30:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Site anchoring & evacuation window — dedicated region assurance

Hi Laura and Ben — adding a few technical details and what we'll provide by EOD Apr 26: 

- Topology: A high-level diagram that highlights tenant boundaries, network ACLs, and control-plane separation (we'll provide site-anchoring-diagram.png).
- Snapshots & replication: Snapshots are stored in the same region by default. Cross-region replication requires an explicit customer opt-in, and it will be called out in the provisioning agreement. Snapshots are tagged with tenant id and retention windows.
- Evacuation mechanics: When we detect a critical host failure we first attempt in-region live eviction and workload rebuild using local warm spares. If those fail and you have opted into cross-region DR, traffic is moved to the configured DR region; otherwise we rely on capacity within the pinned region and temporary burst capacity.
- Auditable evidence: we'll provide a redacted audit extract (events list) and a timeline example from a recent maintenance event showing routing changes and KV cache wipes. These will be scrubbed for customer-identifying data.

We will not include any PII in the artifacts. Dev will coordinate the logs export so it matches your auditor's needs.

Kimberly Park
Head of Dedicated Ops
Redwood Inference

title: Post-demo: token economics + model weighting — action items
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
To: Jordan Lee <jordan.lee@brightai.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.ai>
Date: 2028-04-22T09:15:00-07:00
Subject: Post-demo: token economics + model weighting — action items

Hi Jordan — thanks again for a great demo yesterday. Couple quick notes and requests to make sure we align on pricing and next steps. Summary below followed by three asks.

Recap (short):
- BrightAI demo showed two primary flows: 1) user-facing chat (support + product suggestions) and 2) large-scale content embedding + semantic search for discovery. There is a small reranking/reranker pipeline for recommendation tuning.
- Expected initial steady-state: medium concurrency (10–30 QPS), large embedding throughput for batch imports, and episodic chat spikes around product launches.

Initial volume guidance you shared on the call: ~50M tokens/month baseline with potential to ramp to 300M over 12 months (you said 3–6 month pilot likely in the 50–120M window). Embeddings roughly 1.2M vectors/month initially.

Three asks (so we can produce an accurate pricing estimate and model allocation):
1) Confirm target monthly token volume bands for pilot vs. FY1 target (pick ranges like 0–50M, 50–150M, 150–300M).
2) Share budget range for hosted inference (ballpark $/month) or target $/1M tokens you want to hit. That helps us map to model mix & routing.
3) Confirm priority between latency vs. unit cost for chat. If latency is critical, we route to a lower-latency variant; if cost is priority we suggest higher-quant/cheaper model paths.

Quick initial guidance from our side (to unblock you):
- Pricing reference: example target buckets we commonly use — $600–$1,200 per 1M tokens for low-latency production chat on high-cost model variants; $120–$350 per 1M tokens for quantized cost-optimized chat variants; embeddings typically $0.50–$3 per 1k vectors depending on model/quant; rerank traffic is low-volume and often priced as a small add-on or routed to cheaper rerank-optimized weights.
- Suggested model weighting for a balanced pilot (we'll refine once you confirm volumes): chat 50% of tokens, embeddings 40% of tokens, rerank 10% of tokens. That can shift toward embeddings-heavy if discovery is the core KPI.

Attachments: I attached a short one-pager with example cost math and an editable workbook for per-model splits.

Next steps: can you confirm the three asks above by end of week? We'll produce a formal estimate and a short runbook for pilot SLOs and routing rules. If helpful, Marissa (cc'd) can schedule a pricing/tech sync next week.

Thanks — Ben
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben@redwood.ai | +1 (415) 555-0147
Redwood Inference

Attachments: redwood-pricing-estimate-brief.pdf (1 page), model_mix-proposal-04-22.xlsx (spreadsheet)


From: Jordan Lee <jordan.lee@brightai.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
Cc: procurement@brightai.com
Date: 2028-04-23T11:40:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Post-demo: token economics + model weighting — action items

Hey Ben — thanks for the clear follow-up. Quick answers below to the three asks + a couple clarifying points.

1) Volume bands: pilot = 40–80M tokens/month (likely start in May) — FY1 target = 200–300M tokens/month if the pilot metrics hold.
2) Budget: procurement is looking for ~ $60k–$120k/month during pilot (we can flex), with an FY1 blended target of ~$150k–$400k/month as we ramp. If easier, think of it as a target cost of ~$250–$600 per 1M tokens blended across workloads during pilot.
3) Latency vs cost: for user-facing chat, latency is important (sub-200ms response target for 90th pct on single-turn notifications). Embeddings cost is more important for batch indexing — prioritize cost there.

Model preferences:
- Chat: want a strong conversational model with fast response — we prefer a mid/high capability chat model for customer-facing flows.
- Embeddings: accuracy on semantic search matters more than latency; lower-cost embedding variants OK if we keep good vector quality.
- Rerank: small volume; happy to route to a cheaper rerank model.

Procurement note: they will want an itemized estimate (per-model rates and projected monthly cost) + a standard MSFT-style SLA. I'll loop in the legal/procurement contacts once we have an itemized spreadsheet.

Thanks — Jordan
Jordan Lee
Senior Product Manager, BrightAI
jordan.lee@brightai.com

On Apr 22, 2028, at 9:15 AM, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai> wrote:
> Hi Jordan — thanks again for a great demo yesterday. Couple quick notes and requests to make sure we align on pricing and next steps. Summary below followed by three asks.
>
> [original message trimmed]


From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
To: Jordan Lee <jordan.lee@brightai.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.ai>, procurement@brightai.com
Date: 2028-04-24T08:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Post-demo: token economics + model weighting — action items

Jordan — appreciate the rapid reply and the budget bands. Based on your guidance I put together a quick itemized approach and a starting model-weighting we recommend for the pilot. Attached is a 1-page estimate and the workbook with rows you can edit.

High-level itemized example (pilot, sample math):
- Pilot volume: 60M tokens/month (midpoint).
- Suggested model split (pilot): chat 45% (27M tokens), embeddings 45% (27M tokens), rerank 10% (6M tokens).

Example unit assumptions (for conversation):
- Chat (low-latency variant): $900 / 1M tokens (this is for the mid/high capability variant with reserved low-latency pool).
- Chat (cost-optimized quantized variant): $250 / 1M tokens (used for non-critical back-office prompts).
- Embeddings: $1.25 per 1k vectors (we estimate 1 token ~= 0.3 words; your embeddings volume provided earlier maps to ~1.2M vectors/month).
- Rerank: $120 / 1M tokens (cheap rerank-only weights).

Pilot cost example (blend):
- Chat: 27M tokens @ $900/1M = $24,300
- Embeddings: 27M tokens mapped to 1.2M vectors -> ~ $1,500
- Rerank: 6M tokens @ $120/1M = $720
- Estimated infra & SLO overhead + reserved capacity premium = $7,480
- Total pilot monthly estimate = $34,000 (this sits inside your stated $60k–$120k pilot band; we priced conservatively on reserved low-latency pool)

Notes/assumptions:
- If we increase the proportion of cost-optimized chat paths (quantized variants), blended $/1M token drops significantly (we can show a sensitivity table in the workbook).
- Embeddings scaled pricing and vector dedupe could lower effective costs for you.
- We included a small reserved-capacity premium to maintain the latency SLO. If you prefer no reservation we can do a best-effort routing at cheaper rates but with less strict latency guarantees.

Proposed next steps:
1) I'll update the workbook with 2 sensitivity scenarios (latency-first vs cost-first) and re-send by EOD tomorrow.
2) Marissa — can you prepare a short one-pager describing the canary/rollout plan and suggested SLOs for the pilot?
3) Jordan — if procurement is happy with the itemized estimate we can prep a short-term reservation agreement for pilot capacity.

Forwarded for convenience: attachment + quick math.

Thanks — Ben
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference

text:
From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
To: Jordan Lee <jordan.lee@brightai.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.ai>
Date: 2028-04-22T09:15:00-07:00
Subject: Post-demo: token economics + model weighting — action items

Hi Jordan — thanks again for a great demo yesterday. Couple quick notes and requests to make sure we align on pricing and next steps. Summary below followed by three asks.

Recap (short):
- BrightAI demo showed two primary flows: 1) user-facing chat (support + product suggestions) and 2) large-scale content embedding + semantic search for discovery. There is a small reranking/reranker pipeline for recommendation tuning.
- Expected initial steady-state: medium concurrency (10–30 QPS), large embedding throughput for batch imports, and episodic chat spikes around product launches.

Initial volume guidance you shared on the call: ~50M tokens/month baseline with potential to ramp to 300M over 12 months (you said 3–6 month pilot likely in the 50–120M window). Embeddings roughly 1.2M vectors/month initially.

Three asks (so we can produce an accurate pricing estimate and model allocation):
1) Confirm target monthly token volume bands for pilot vs. FY1 target (pick ranges like 0–50M, 50–150M, 150–300M).
2) Share budget range for hosted inference (ballpark $/month) or target $/1M tokens you want to hit. That helps us map to model mix & routing.
3) Confirm priority between latency vs. unit cost for chat. If latency is critical, we route to a lower-latency variant; if cost is priority we suggest higher-quant/cheaper model paths.

Quick initial guidance from our side (to unblock you):
- Pricing reference: example target buckets we commonly use — $600–$1,200 per 1M tokens for low-latency production chat on high-cost model variants; $120–$350 per 1M tokens for quantized cost-optimized chat variants; embeddings typically $0.50–$3 per 1k vectors depending on model/quant; rerank traffic is low-volume and often priced as a small add-on or routed to cheaper rerank-optimized weights.
- Suggested model weighting for a balanced pilot (we'll refine once you confirm volumes): chat 50% of tokens, embeddings 40% of tokens, rerank 10% of tokens. That can shift toward embeddings-heavy if discovery is the core KPI.

Attachments: I attached a short one-pager with example cost math and an editable workbook for per-model splits.

Next steps: can you confirm the three asks above by end of week? We'll produce a formal estimate and a short runbook for pilot SLOs and routing rules. If helpful, Marissa (cc'd) can schedule a pricing/tech sync next week.

Thanks — Ben
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben@redwood.ai | +1 (415) 555-0147
Redwood Inference

Attachments: redwood-pricing-estimate-brief.pdf (1 page), model_mix-proposal-04-22.xlsx (spreadsheet)


From: Jordan Lee <jordan.lee@brightai.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
Cc: procurement@brightai.com
Date: 2028-04-23T11:40:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Post-demo: token economics + model weighting — action items

Hey Ben — thanks for the clear follow-up. Quick answers below to the three asks + a couple clarifying points.

1) Volume bands: pilot = 40–80M tokens/month (likely start in May) — FY1 target = 200–300M tokens/month if the pilot metrics hold.
2) Budget: procurement is looking for ~ $60k–$120k/month during pilot (we can flex), with an FY1 blended target of ~$150k–$400k/month as we ramp. If easier, think of it as a target cost of ~$250–$600 per 1M tokens blended across workloads during pilot.
3) Latency vs cost: for user-facing chat, latency is important (sub-200ms response target for 90th pct on single-turn notifications). Embeddings cost is more important for batch indexing — prioritize cost there.

Model preferences:
- Chat: want a strong conversational model with fast response — we prefer a mid/high capability chat model for customer-facing flows.
- Embeddings: accuracy on semantic search matters more than latency; lower-cost embedding variants OK if we keep good vector quality.
- Rerank: small volume; happy to route to a cheaper rerank model.

Procurement note: they will want an itemized estimate (per-model rates and projected monthly cost) + a standard MSFT-style SLA. I'll loop in the legal/procurement contacts once we have an itemized spreadsheet.

Subject: Re: Post-demo: token economics + model weighting — action items

Jordan — appreciate the rapid reply and the budget bands. Based on your guidance I put together a quick itemized approach and a starting model-weighting we recommend for the pilot. Attached is a 1-page estimate and the workbook with rows you can edit.

High-level itemized example (pilot, sample math):
- Pilot volume: 60M tokens/month (midpoint).
- Suggested model split (pilot): chat 45% (27M tokens), embeddings 45% (27M tokens), rerank 10% (6M tokens).

Example unit assumptions (for conversation):
- Chat (low-latency variant): $900 / 1M tokens (this is for the mid/high capability variant with reserved low-latency pool).
- Chat (cost-optimized quantized variant): $250 / 1M tokens (used for non-critical back-office prompts).
- Embeddings: $1.25 per 1k vectors (we estimate 1 token ~= 0.3 words; your embeddings volume provided earlier maps to ~1.2M vectors/month).
- Rerank: $120 / 1M tokens (cheap rerank-only weights).

Pilot cost example (blend):
- Chat: 27M tokens @ $900/1M = $24,300
- Embeddings: 27M tokens mapped to 1.2M vectors -> ~ $1,500
- Rerank: 6M tokens @ $120/1M = $720
- Estimated infra & SLO overhead + reserved capacity premium = $7,480
- Total pilot monthly estimate = $34,000 (this sits inside your stated $60k–$120k pilot band; we priced conservatively on reserved low-latency pool)

Notes/assumptions:
- If we increase the proportion of cost-optimized chat paths (quantized variants), blended $/1M token drops significantly (we can show a sensitivity table in the workbook).
- Embeddings scaled pricing and vector dedupe could lower effective costs for you.
- We included a small reserved-capacity premium to maintain the latency SLO. If you prefer no reservation we can do a best-effort routing at cheaper rates but with less strict latency guarantees.

Proposed next steps:
1) I'll update the workbook with 2 sensitivity scenarios (latency-first vs cost-first) and re-send by EOD tomorrow.
2) Marissa — can you prepare a short one-pager describing the canary/rollout plan and suggested SLOs for the pilot?
3) Jordan — if procurement is happy with the itemized estimate we can prep a short-term reservation agreement for pilot capacity.

Forwarded for convenience: attachment + quick math.

Thanks — Ben

title: Request: Nightly Throughput Stability Waiver for Dedicated Pool - Aurorix
preview:
From: Claire Benton <claire@aurorix.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-04-20T17:03:00Z
Subject: Nightly batch spikes & Dedicated pool throughput waiver request

Hi Ava,

Thanks again for the walkthrough this morning. Short version: our production model traffic is dominated by user inference during the day, but we also run a large nightly batch that backs the recommendation index (approx. 50M tokens in a 90-minute window). Over the last two months we've seen that this batch occasionally pushes our reserved GPUs well above the concurrency profile assumed in the contract, which triggers preemption and tail latency hits for live traffic.

What we are asking for: a time-boxed carveout (nightly 02:00–03:30 UTC, Mon/Wed/Fri) where the dedicated pool will allow short bursts above the usual per-tenant throughput floor without triggering aggressive preemption, in exchange for a firm committed reserve level for the rest of the day. The carveout would be limited to three windows/week for 12 weeks while we migrate parts of the batch to a cheaper offline job.

We attached a workload profile and a tentative schedule (aurorix_workload_profile.xlsx). Specific asks:
- Allow up to 2x burst throughput for 90 minutes on the listed pool during the carveout windows.
- If live latency exceeds SLA during the carveout, apply a pre-defined fallback to a smaller model (we can accept a fallback to the 7B variant).
- We request retroactive mitigation credits for the two incidents in March (we can provide traces).

Can Redwood operations review and tell us what operational safeguards you’d need (monitoring thresholds, fallback triggers, token/connection limits) and whether this would be acceptable as an exception to the standard throughput guarantee?

Appreciate it,
Claire
Head of Platform Engineering, Aurorix

(Attachment: aurorix_workload_profile.xlsx)

From: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
To: Claire Benton <claire@aurorix.ai>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, David Kim <david@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-04-20T18:12:00Z
Subject: Re: Nightly batch spikes & Dedicated pool throughput waiver request

Claire —

Thanks for the clear ask and for sharing the profile. I appreciate the time-boxing approach — that makes this tractable. I'm looping in Ben (sales lead on the Aurorix deal) and David (our SRE/solutions lead) to scope the operational and commercial tradeoffs. Next steps I propose:

1) Internal ops review to map the burst window to a technical playbook (monitoring, fallback levels, burst token budget).
2) Commercial proposal with a short-term pricing uplift for burst headroom + clear credit caps for any SLA misses.
3) Legal/contract amendment outlining time-box, duration (12 weeks), and rollback conditions.

We should have an internal recommendation in ~5 business days. Ben/David — can you confirm availability to review the workload sheet and propose a pilot plan?

Ava Chen
Account Executive, Redwood Inference

-- Original message attached for context --


From: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, David Kim <david@redwood.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-04-21T14:05:00Z
Subject: FWD: Nightly batch spikes & Dedicated pool throughput waiver request

Folks — forwarding Aurorix's request. Quick internal notes (technical + commercial):

Ben — commercial signal: customer keeps committed reserve for business hours but needs burst headroom at night. They are offering to accept model fallback (7B) during any live-impacting events and are willing to pay an uplift for the burst windows. They also asked for retro credits for two March incidents — please advise on precedent and limits.

David — tech questions we need to answer from an ops POV:
- Can we implement a "burst token" accounting scheme scoped to the dedicated pool (e.g., X extra inflight tokens per window) that prevents noisy neighbors but still allows the batch to finish?
- For live path protection, can we: 1) prefactor model priming (KV cache warmup), 2) reserve a small low-latency lane for <N concurrent live requests, and 3) automatically trigger fallback to 7B when tail latency threshold crosses 95th pctl > Y ms for >Z seconds?
- What monitoring and alerting thresholds do we need to safely allow this without manual intervention?

Lauren — legal/product: if we accept a time-boxed waiver, we should draft an amendment that:
- Defines the carveout windows and max burst budget (both absolute tokens and duration).
- Caps credits to a fixed percentage of monthly fees for the trial period (to avoid open-ended liability).

Hi Sara — thanks again for the demo this morning. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we keep momentum: 

1) Quickstart scope (what we propose to provision for Clearspring trial):
   - Hosted sandbox endpoints (streaming + non-streaming)
   - 2 sandbox API keys, 30-day trial credits, and a per-endpoint rate slice for your integration tests

title: AsterionBio dedicated capacity proposal and throughput safety margin
preview:
From: Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.com>
To: Emma Jones <emma.jones@asterionbio.com>, Samuel Choi <samuel.choi@asterionbio.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 20 Apr 2028 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Dedicated capacity proposal for AsterionBio — initial sizing and questions

Hi Emma / Samuel,

Thanks again for the demo yesterday — great to understand the mix of short clinical prompts + longer summarization (average response length ~420 tokens). As promised, I attached an initial throughput model (asterion_throughput_model.xlsx) that maps your stated traffic profile to recommended Dedicated capacity and a small burst pool. Quick summary of the model and a couple of questions so we can lock in sizing: 

Key numbers we used (please confirm):
- Average tokens per generation: 420 tokens
- Peak concurrent requests: 120 RPS at peak minute windows (you mentioned a top-of-hour spike pattern)
- Expected p95 response length during spikes: 700 tokens

Redwood runtime assumptions used in the model:
- Model planned to serve: Llama-3-70B (8-bit quantized) with Redwood kernel optimizations
- Measured throughput per 80GB GPU (baseline A100-like profile in our testbed): 130 tokens/sec sustained with small batching (typical 8–16 req batch)
- Target utilization for Dedicated slab sizing: 70% (we leave room for KV cache warmup, background jobs, and scheduling jitter)
- Safety margin (headroom) we recommend: 35% for first 90 days while you ramp and stabilize

Sizing math (short):
- Peak tokens/sec estimate = peak RPS * p95 tokens = 120 * 700 = 84,000 tokens/sec
- Effective tokens/sec per GPU (after util & safety) = 130 * 0.70 * (1 - 0.35) ≈ 59 tokens/sec
- GPUs required (rounded up) = ceil(84,000 / 59) ≈ 1,424 GPUs

That number is intentionally conservative and assumes the heavier summarization workload and a large safety margin. The spreadsheet includes alternate scenarios (smaller safety margin, quantization to 4-bit, caching gains for repeated prefixes) which bring the GPU need down substantially.

Asks / clarifications we need to finalize:
1) Can you confirm the peak minute RPS number (120) and whether the 120 is sustained across a 60s window or a sub-10s microburst?
2) Do you have an expected distribution of token lengths (e.g., % of requests under 128 tokens, 128–512, 512–1,000, >1,000)? That materially affects batching efficiency and KV cache benefits.
3) Any hard p95 latency SLO you must meet for production (e.g., 300ms, 500ms)? Our sizing trades off tokens/sec capacity for lower tail latency.

Next steps: once you confirm (1)–(3) we’ll lock a recommended slab configuration and a proposed commitment tier. I’ve looped Jordan (pricing) and Priyom (inference runtime / ops) to weigh in on tradeoffs — Priyom will propose an optimized configuration that reduces GPU count by applying burst pooling, prefix caching, and quantization recommendations.

Thanks,

Camila Reyes
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
camila_reyes@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0123
Redwood Inference

Attachments: asterion_throughput_model.xlsx (spreadsheet), redwood-dedicated-proposal.pdf (proposal stub)

From: Samuel Choi <samuel.choi@asterionbio.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.com>, Emma Jones <emma.jones@asterionbio.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 20 Apr 2028 10:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity proposal for AsterionBio — initial sizing and questions

Camila — thanks, this is helpful and ambitious. A couple of clarifications from our side: 

- The 120 RPS number is the worst-case top-of-hour spike measured over a 10s window (so it’s a short burst, not sustained for the full minute). We have a broader background of ~18 RPS steady-state.
- Token distribution (rough approximate): ~55% under 256 tokens (short lookups), ~30% in 256–700, ~12% 700–1600 (long summaries), ~3% occasional >1600 when returning long patient history aggregations.
- Our latency SLO: p95 <= 450ms for text-only endpoints. For the long summarization flows we are willing to accept p95 <= 900ms if necessary (the user flow is async).

Also: we are open to hybrid strategies — a Dedicated slab for predictable baseline and a hosted burst pool for those 10s spikes. Curious how you modeled burst pools and warm-start vs. cold starts in the spreadsheet.

Sam
Senior Engineering Manager, AsterionBio
samuel.choi@asterionbio.com

-----Original message-----
On Fri, Apr 20, 2028 at 09:12 AM Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.com> wrote:
> (see above)


From: Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.com>, Samuel Choi <samuel.choi@asterionbio.com>, Emma Jones <emma.jones@asterionbio.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 20 Apr 2028 11:18:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity proposal for AsterionBio — initial sizing and questions

Thanks for the details, Sam — that clarifies a lot. A few technical notes and an alternative sizing approach that will reduce the 1,400+ GPU topline estimate substantially: 

1) Burst vs sustained math: because the 120 RPS is a 10s microburst, you don’t need to commit 1,400 GPUs for the whole time window. Two levers help: a) a small dedicated slab sized for the sustained baseline (we’ll size for 18 RPS steady-state), b) a shared hosted burst pool (or a short-term elastic burst slab) that covers the 10s spikes.

2) Effective tokens/sec per GPU by request mix: the 130 tokens/sec baseline we shared assumes long 700-token responses are dominant. With your mix (55% <256), our internal microbench shows an average tokens/sec of ~210 tokens/sec per GPU when batching is efficient across mixed lengths (the shorter requests pack multiple per batch).

3) Recompute with pragmatic headroom:
- Peak tokens/sec instantaneous for 10s burst = 120 RPS * 700 tokens = 84,000 tokens/sec (same as Camila’s calc)
- Sustained baseline tokens/sec = 18 RPS * 420 avg = 7,560 tokens/sec
- Effective per-GPU tokens/sec (mixed profile, 70% util, 25% safety margin) = 210 * 0.70 * (1 - 0.25) ≈ 110 tokens/sec

Sizing recommendation (hybrid):
- Dedicated baseline slab: size to meet sustained baseline with room for steady growth: GPUs_baseline = ceil(7,560 / 110) = 69 GPUs
- Burst coverage (short 10s spikes): incremental GPUs needed = ceil((84,000 - (69 * 210 * 0.70)) / 110) ≈ ceil((84,000 - 10,143) / 110) ≈ 677 GPUs transient.

Operational notes:
- We can satisfy spike coverage by a mix of: (A) hosted burst pool (pay-as-you-go) + (B) short-term refundable burst slab (30–90 day) — both approaches reduce your upfront committed GPUs.
- Additional levers to lower total GPUs: prefix caching (for repeated clinical query patterns), 4-bit quantization (adds ~25–40% tokens/sec per GPU in our tests for this model class), and request-level batching tweaks.

I’ve updated the spreadsheet with scenario tabs for: baseline-only, hybrid-burst, and high-quant. If you want, I can run a stress test in our test cluster with a representative sample payload next week to validate the 210 tokens/sec estimate.

Priyom Das
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
priyom_das@redwood.com

text:
From: Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.com>
To: Emma Jones <emma.jones@asterionbio.com>, Samuel Choi <samuel.choi@asterionbio.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 20 Apr 2028 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Dedicated capacity proposal for AsterionBio — initial sizing and questions

Hi Emma / Samuel,

Thanks again for the demo yesterday — great to understand the mix of short clinical prompts + longer summarization (average response length ~420 tokens). As promised, I attached an initial throughput model (asterion_throughput_model.xlsx) that maps your stated traffic profile to recommended Dedicated capacity and a small burst pool. Quick summary of the model and a couple of questions so we can lock in sizing: 

Key numbers we used (please confirm):
- Average tokens per generation: 420 tokens
- Peak concurrent requests: 120 RPS at peak minute windows (you mentioned a top-of-hour spike pattern)
- Expected p95 response length during spikes: 700 tokens

Redwood runtime assumptions used in the model:
- Model planned to serve: Llama-3-70B (8-bit quantized) with Redwood kernel optimizations
- Measured throughput per 80GB GPU (baseline A100-like profile in our testbed): 130 tokens/sec sustained with small batching (typical 8–16 req batch)
- Target utilization for Dedicated slab sizing: 70% (we leave room for KV cache warmup, background jobs, and scheduling jitter)
- Safety margin (headroom) we recommend: 35% for first 90 days while you ramp and stabilize

Sizing math (short):
- Peak tokens/sec estimate = peak RPS * p95 tokens = 120 * 700 = 84,000 tokens/sec
- Effective tokens/sec per GPU (after util & safety) = 130 * 0.70 * (1 - 0.35) ≈ 59 tokens/sec
- GPUs required (rounded up) = ceil(84,000 / 59) ≈ 1,424 GPUs

That number is intentionally conservative and assumes the heavier summarization workload and a large safety margin. The spreadsheet includes alternate scenarios (smaller safety margin, quantization to 4-bit, caching gains for repeated prefixes) which bring the GPU need down substantially.

Asks / clarifications we need to finalize:
1) Can you confirm the peak minute RPS number (120) and whether the 120 is sustained across a 60s window or a sub-10s microburst?
2) Do you have an expected distribution of token lengths (e.g., % of requests under 128 tokens, 128–512, 512–1,000, >1,000)? That materially affects batching efficiency and KV cache benefits.
3) Any hard p95 latency SLO you must meet for production (e.g., 300ms, 500ms)? Our sizing trades off tokens/sec capacity for lower tail latency.

Next steps: once you confirm (1)–(3) we’ll lock a recommended slab configuration and a proposed commitment tier. I’ve looped Jordan (pricing) and Priyom (inference runtime / ops) to weigh in on tradeoffs — Priyom will propose an optimized configuration that reduces GPU count by applying burst pooling, prefix caching, and quantization recommendations.

Thanks,

Camila Reyes
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
camila_reyes@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0123
Redwood Inference

Attachments: asterion_throughput_model.xlsx (spreadsheet), redwood-dedicated-proposal.pdf (proposal stub)

From: Samuel Choi <samuel.choi@asterionbio.com>

Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 20 Apr 2028 10:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity proposal for AsterionBio — initial sizing and questions

Camila — thanks, this is helpful and ambitious. A couple of clarifications from our side: 

- The 120 RPS number is the worst-case top-of-hour spike measured over a 10s window (so it’s a short burst, not sustained for the full minute). We have a broader background of ~18 RPS steady-state.
- Token distribution (rough approximate): ~55% under 256 tokens (short lookups), ~30% in 256–700, ~12% 700–1600 (long summaries), ~3% occasional >1600 when returning long patient history aggregations.
- Our latency SLO: p95 <= 450ms for text-only endpoints. For the long summarization flows we are willing to accept p95 <= 900ms if necessary (the user flow is async).

Also: we are open to hybrid strategies — a Dedicated slab for predictable baseline and a hosted burst pool for those 10s spikes. Curious how you modeled burst pools and warm-start vs. cold starts in the spreadsheet.

Sam
Senior Engineering Manager, AsterionBio
samuel.choi@asterionbio.com

-----Original message-----
On Fri, Apr 20, 2028 at 09:12 AM Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.com> wrote:
> (see above)


From: Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.com>, Samuel Choi <samuel.choi@asterionbio.com>, Emma Jones <emma.jones@asterionbio.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 20 Apr 2028 11:18:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity proposal for AsterionBio — initial sizing and questions

Thanks for the details, Sam — that clarifies a lot. A few technical notes and an alternative sizing approach that will reduce the 1,400+ GPU topline estimate substantially: 

1) Burst vs sustained math: because the 120 RPS is a 10s microburst, you don’t need to commit 1,400 GPUs for the whole time window. Two levers help: a) a small dedicated slab sized for the sustained baseline (we’ll size for 18 RPS steady-state), b) a shared hosted burst pool (or a short-term elastic burst slab) that covers the 10s spikes.

2) Effective tokens/sec per GPU by request mix: the 130 tokens/sec baseline we shared assumes long 700-token responses are dominant. With your mix (55% <256), our internal microbench shows an average tokens/sec of ~210 tokens/sec per GPU when batching is efficient across mixed lengths (the shorter requests pack multiple per batch).

3) Recompute with pragmatic headroom:
- Peak tokens/sec instantaneous for 10s burst = 120 RPS * 700 tokens = 84,000 tokens/sec (same as Camila’s calc)
- Sustained baseline tokens/sec = 18 RPS * 420 avg = 7,560 tokens/sec
- Effective per-GPU tokens/sec (mixed profile, 70% util, 25% safety margin) = 210 * 0.70 * (1 - 0.25) ≈ 110 tokens/sec

Sizing recommendation (hybrid):
- Dedicated baseline slab: size to meet sustained baseline with room for steady growth: GPUs_baseline = ceil(7,560 / 110) = 69 GPUs
- Burst coverage (short 10s spikes): incremental GPUs needed = ceil((84,000 - (69 * 210 * 0.70)) / 110) ≈ ceil((84,000 - 10,143) / 110) ≈ 677 GPUs transient.

Operational notes:
- We can satisfy spike coverage by a mix of: (A) hosted burst pool (pay-as-you-go) + (B) short-term refundable burst slab (30–90 day) — both approaches reduce your upfront committed GPUs.
- Additional levers to lower total GPUs: prefix caching (for repeated clinical query patterns), 4-bit quantization (adds ~25–40% tokens/sec per GPU in our tests for this model class), and request-level batching tweaks.

I’ve updated the spreadsheet with scenario tabs for: baseline-only, hybrid-burst, and high-quant. If you want, I can run a stress test in our test cluster with a representative sample payload next week to validate the 210 tokens/sec estimate.

Priyom Das
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
priyom_das@redwood.com
